key_points: The chapter introduces spectacular natural wonders that highlight the beauty and diversity of nature
The Valley of Flowers in Uttarakhand is known for its wide range of alpine flowers and unique Himalayan ecosystem
The valley supports many species of plants, insects, and animals, making it a biodiversity hotspot
Living Root Bridges in Meghalaya are created by guiding the roots of rubber trees across rivers
These bridges reflect the traditional ecological knowledge of local communities
examples: Hundreds of flower species bloom in the Valley of Flowers during the monsoon season
Animals such as the red fox live in the valley’s ecosystem
Villagers carefully guide the roots of rubber trees for many years to create Living Root Bridges
Some Living Root Bridges are more than a hundred years old and are still used by people
Responsible tourism practices help protect these natural wonders
formulas_or_processes: Natural Ecosystem → Plants + Animals + Climate + Environment
Living Root Bridge Formation → Guide tree roots → Roots grow and intertwine → Strong natural bridge forms
Environmental Protection → Awareness + Conservation + Responsible tourism
Human–Nature Harmony → Using natural resources without harming the environment
exam_tips: Understand the ecological importance of the Valley of Flowers
Explain how Living Root Bridges are formed using tree roots
Remember the meaning and importance of biodiversity
Focus on how traditional knowledge helps protect the environment
Link the examples in the chapter to the idea of sustainable interaction with nature
